backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
Tibialis anterior is the most superficial muscle. It dorsiflexes and inverts the foot.

Clinical Pearl: Weakness causes foot drop — the hallmark of common peroneal nerve injury.

Memory Aid: "Tibialis Anterior = Turns Ankle inward."

The deep peroneal nerve (L4-S2) is the motor nerve of the entire anterior compartment. Sensory supply: only the first web space.

Clinical Pearl: Testing 1st web space sensation is a quick screen for deep peroneal nerve integrity.

Memory Aid: "Deep = Dorsiflexors."

The anterior tibial artery passes through the interosseous membrane and becomes the dorsalis pedis artery at the ankle.

Clinical Pearl: The dorsalis pedis pulse is key in peripheral vascular disease and diabetic foot assessment.

Memory Aid: "ATA goes Anterior Through A gap."

EDL splits into 4 tendons inserting into the middle and distal phalanges of toes 2-5.

Clinical Pearl: In anterior compartment syndrome, pain on passive toe flexion is an early warning sign — surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy.

Memory Aid: "EDL = Every Digit Lifted."

The anterior compartment is enclosed by tibia, fibula, interosseous membrane, and deep fascia — a closed osseofascial space.

Clinical Pearl: The anterior compartment is most commonly affected by acute compartment syndrome. Pressure >30 mmHg warrants emergency fasciotomy.

Memory Aid: "4 walls, no room to swell."
